title: Question 1 (Rao–Shinkar Question 6.1)
statement_text: Is there a mapping from Dictator to XOR that is $O(1)$-local and $O(1)$-bi-Lipschitz?
context_text: Rao and Shinkar constructed a map from Dictator to XOR that is 2-Lipschitz and 3-local but whose inverse is only $O(\log n)$-Lipschitz, and asked whether an $O(1)$-bi-Lipschitz and $O(1)$-local map could exist. The question concerns the interplay between locality and bi-Lipschitz stretch for mappings between these two boolean functions.
paper_contribution: Answers in the negative (Theorem 1): any $C$-Lipschitz mapping from Dictator to XOR where each output bit depends on at most $k$ input bits has inverse that is not $\delta\log(n)$-Lipschitz for $\delta = \Omega(1/(k+\log(C)))$, so no $O(1)$-local $O(1)$-bi-Lipschitz map can exist.

title: Question 2 (Rao–Shinkar Question 6.3)
statement_text: Is there a mapping $\phi$ from XOR to Majority such that $\mathrm{avgStretch}(\phi) = O(\sqrt{n})$?
context_text: Rao and Shinkar proved that any mapping from XOR to Majority must have average stretch $\Omega(\sqrt{n})$ in any direction, and asked whether this lower bound is tight. The question concerns the optimal average stretch achievable by a bijection between these two boolean functions.
paper_contribution: Answers in the positive (Theorem 2): constructs an explicit bijection from XOR to Majority via a symmetric chain decomposition argument that achieves average stretch $O(\sqrt{n})$.

title: Question 3 (Rao–Shinkar Question 6.4)
statement_text: Is there a Lipschitz embedding $\phi : \{0,1\}^n \to \{0,1\}^{\mathrm{poly}(n)}$ such that $\mathrm{XOR}(x) = \mathrm{Majority}(\phi(x))$ for all $x \in \{0,1\}^n$?
context_text: Since any bijection from XOR to Majority requires large average stretch ($\Omega(\sqrt{n})$), Rao and Shinkar asked whether the problem becomes easier if one relaxes from a bijection on $\{0,1\}^n$ to an injection into a polynomially larger hypercube, allowing a $O(1)$-Lipschitz solution.
paper_contribution: Answers in the positive (Theorem 3): constructs a 3-Lipschitz embedding $\phi : \{0,1\}^n \to \{0,1\}^{2n+1}$ defined by $\phi(x) = (x^c, \mathrm{XOR}(x), x)$ such that $\mathrm{XOR}(x) = \mathrm{Majority}(\phi(x))$ for all $x \in \{0,1\}^n$.

title: Question 4 (Rao–Shinkar Question 6.2)
statement_text: Is it true that with high probability there is a $O(1)$-bi-Lipschitz mapping from Dictator to a uniformly random balanced function $f$?
context_text: Building on a construction of Håstad, Leighton and Newman, Rao and Shinkar showed that with high probability there is a bijection from Dictator to a uniformly random balanced function with $O(1)$ average stretch, and asked whether the stronger property of $O(1)$-bi-Lipschitz can also be guaranteed with high probability.
paper_contribution: Answers in the positive (Theorem 4): proves that with high probability there exists a mapping $\phi$ from Dictator to $f$ satisfying $\mathrm{dist}(x,\phi(x)) \leq C$ for an absolute constant $C$ and all $x$, which implies $\phi$ is $(2C+1)$-bi-Lipschitz.

title: Informal Question (Section 2)
statement_text: Does there exist a map $\phi$ from Dictator to XOR which is $C$-Lipschitz, where each output bit depends on at most $k$ input bits, and such that $\phi^{-1}$ is $o(\log n / \log k)$-Lipschitz as $n, k \to \infty$?
context_text: Theorem 1 shows that for general maps the inverse must be at least $\Omega(1/k)\cdot\log n$-Lipschitz, while for linear maps the bound is $\Omega(1/\log k)\cdot\log n$-Lipschitz. Rao and Shinkar's construction achieves $\delta(2,k)=O(1/\log k)$ for linear maps, matching the linear-map lower bound, but a gap remains for general maps between $\Omega(1/k)$ and $O(1/\log k)$.
notes: Stated in running prose as 'This raises the following question:' without a labelled theorem environment.
